data.title: Binance Life: A 15-Fold Increase to a New High – Three Lifelines Saved by an Artificial Bull Market
data.description:
CZ's new book, "Binance Life," has been released, and the token of the same name has begun a violent upward trend, reaching the high point of this round of price increases, rising 15 times from the low point at the end of March.

This is a 15-fold increase at a time when cryptocurrency trading volume is dismal, the wealth effect is shrinking sharply, Bitcoin has been halved from its peak, and simply put, the cryptocurrency market is at the bottom of the technology asset industry.

The 15x increase brought a wealth effect far greater than that of "Binance Life" alone. On Binance, altcoins saw a long-awaited 24-hour doubling in price, and familiar fund rotations began to appear, such as inscription, Chinese memes, or Polkadot-related coins. The "Binance Life" price surge slightly revitalized the crypto market, which had hit rock bottom. This happened again on Binance. This artificial bull market in a bear market has occurred twice before, precisely when it was most needed. On January 28, 2019, BTT opened for subscription on Binance Launchpad, with the window lasting 18 minutes. The $7.12 million allocation sold out in 18 minutes. The offering price was $0.00012, and eight days later it had increased more than tenfold. That was a time when everyone was lost and directionless during a super bear market. Binance Launchpad solved a very direct problem: giving ordinary investors an entry point to acquire early access to tokens. This had never happened before. If you bought it, you were one step ahead of others. The scarcity was real, and FOMO (Fear of Missing Out) arose naturally. In March 2022, BTC fell 34% from its all-time high of $68,700 to $37,800. While most investors were still undecided whether to exit or continue waiting, STEPN's governance token, GMT, completed its subscription on Binance Launchpad at an offering price of $0.01. On April 24th, GMT was trading at $4.1144, a 411-fold increase from its offering price, with a peak circulating market capitalization of $2.46 billion. Google Trends data showed that global search interest for "STEPN" reached a perfect score of 100 that week, almost coinciding with the price peak. In June, daily active users reached 1 million. Copycat projects subsequently emerged, with at least 17 Move-to-earn projects launching. Compared to BTT, GMT's narrative is on a higher level. BTT sells scarcity. GMT sells a story supported by behavioral logic: earning money by walking—explainable and shareable. It accurately captured two collective emotions in the post-pandemic era: a yearning for outdoor activities and a fantasy of crypto returns. A Binance-backed platform + a compelling product narrative = the conditions for igniting a firestorm in a bear market. Four years later, when the crypto community desperately needed something to shake things up, "Binance Life" appeared. Coupled with the release of Changpeng Zhao's new book and the launch of a nationwide reading week, old stories were reopened, generating sustained media buzz and pushing meme coins to a new all-time high. BTT needed a trading protocol, GMT needed a pair of sneakers and a mobile app. Binance Life needed only a sentence, a meme, and a book title. In terms of process, the "Binance Life" event wasn't as organic as previous ones. Veteran investors will surely remember the IEO where delivery riders would skip 8 PM to grab a share of the new coin; the "running to earn money" update directly redefined Web3 and became a trend. This time, there were basically no newcomers in the market, but coupled with a popular new book, the volatile coins on Binance brought long-awaited altcoin volatility, and in terms of results, it seems more satisfying than the previous two. This is certainly not a macro bull market; money hasn't yet flowed to Bitcoin, let alone altcoins. But even if it's a short-lived, artificial bull market, it's surprisingly moving.

data.title: Hyperliquid has become the top interest of traditional hedge funds.
data.description:
Source: Empire Podcast - Markets Bounce Back, The Hyperliquid Thesis and Kraken Raises $200M

Host:

Jason Yanowitz (Co-founder of Blockworks)

Santiago Roel Santos (Santi, crypto investor, former partner of ParaFi Capital)

Editor's Note

Santi makes a cautionary statement on the show: This is the most intense interest in the crypto industry from traditional hedge fund managers since Paul Tudor Jones called Bitcoin "the fastest horse on the track" in May 2020, and this time the focus is not on BTC, but on Hyperliquid.

The catalyst is the Iranian conflict.

Commodities need a pricing venue over the weekend when traditional markets are closed. Trade XYZ's median error in predicting weekend oil prices to Monday's opening price was only 50 basis points, which boosted Hyperliquid's commodity open interest to 17%, surpassing Ethereum. Santi predicts that commodity trading volume will exceed Bitcoin this year, and Hyperliquid's market capitalization is expected to jump from $25 billion to $100 billion. Two other key indicators: Kraken's valuation has shrunk from $20 billion at the end of 2025 to $13.3 billion; Deutsche Börse invested $200 million for a 1.5% stake (the discount may stem from the structural difference between common and preferred stock); and the SEC clarified this week that self-custodied DeFi front-ends (MetaMask, Phantom, etc.) are not bound by broker-dealer registration rules, resolving one of the biggest unresolved legal issues in the DeFi world.

Key Quotes

Hyperliquid and Weekend Markets

This is the most interest I've seen from traditional hedge fund managers in crypto since Paul Tudor Jones called Bitcoin the fastest horse on the track in May 2020, and that interest is focused on Hyperliquid. Weekend markets are a big reason for this. (Santi)

Hyperliquid essentially moves the entire CME matching engine, order book, clearinghouse, and margin system onto an integrated blockchain, with every transaction, clearing, and funding fee recorded in an immutable public ledger. (Santi)

My prediction: Commodity trading volume on Hyperliquid will surpass Bitcoin's this year.

It currently has a market capitalization of $25 billion, and I believe there's a clear path to $100 billion. (Santi) I give it a 30% to 40% chance of obtaining US compliance approval. If it does get approved, it will be an instant re-rating. CME's market capitalization is $117 billion, and the market always needs a benchmark. (Jason) Hyperliquid's Institutional Adoption Path I received a call this week from a large hedge fund manager asking me to explain Hyperliquid to him. Their team spends most of their time on Twitter, and their trading inspiration also comes from Twitter. Since their fund can't directly buy HYPE tokens, they bought Hyperliquid's DAT (Digital Asset Treasury), which is now one of their largest holdings. He said this was one of the few assets he could buy that wasn't related to AI trading. (Jason) Trading Philosophy and Personal Positions My core logic for continuously adding to my positions is based on my own consumption patterns. The companies where I spend the most money year after year (Amazon, DoorDash) are the targets I add to my positions in. A senior colleague once said that the younger you are, the more alpha you can gain from being "close to the front lines," and in the future, there will be less and less alpha on the internet; you have to discover it by seeing it with your own eyes and using it yourself. (Jason) The Link Between Macro and Crypto The past 10 trading days have been one of the most unusual ten days since 1950. The S&P 500 rose 9.8%, placing it at the 99.7th percentile of all historical 10-day gains. (Santi) I think the strong performance of crypto is due to a good macro environment and a good stock market; the correlation is very strong. Bitcoin hit $75,000 mainly because the market bet that the probability of the Clarity Act passing has increased from 50/50 last week to 60% to 65% now. (Jason) Regulatory Shift The SEC told MetaMask, Phantom, and basically all DeFi front-ends this week: You are not brokers. This ends one of the biggest legal mysteries in DeFi. (Jason) L2 and the "Melting Ice Block" The L2 sector has been like a melting ice block for the past year or so. In 2017, you could sell dreams even without users or data; today, the crypto market's patience for white paper-style narratives is almost zero. (Santi) I think this round will really see a batch of protocols go to zero, not just fading into oblivion like in the past, but the market simply abandoning them. Funds will only concentrate on truly successful assets like Hyperliquid and Bitcoin. (Santi) Next Round of Opportunities My three main investment themes are: demand for stablecoins outside the US will continue to expand and is far from being met; the world will only become more volatile; and there is a serious global affordability crisis. These three pillars all have corresponding implementation paths on-chain. (Santi) Last year's three hottest topics were derivatives, prediction markets, and stablecoins. Projects doing these three things were able to secure funding and users, while projects outside these areas were crushed. This year there will be three new things, but I don't know what they are yet. (Jason) I guess one of the three things that happened this year was tokenization, especially the attempts made by traditional issuers on public permissionless networks. (Santi) III. Full Dialogue Opening: Podcast Data Review and Content Differentiation in the AI ​​Era Santi: This is the most interest I've seen from traditional hedge fund managers in crypto since Paul Tudor Jones (a well-known macro hedge fund manager) called Bitcoin "the fastest horse on the track" in May 2020, and that interest is focused on Hyperliquid (an on-chain perpetual contract exchange). The weekend market was a big reason for that. Jason: Hello listeners, welcome back to Empire Weekly. Santi, how have you been lately? Santi: Nothing special, just happy to be back on the show. A lot is happening in the crypto market and the world at large; it's good to live in the moment. Jason: Santi's been stealing our producer's job lately. He imported all the Empire data into Claude, using the new Opus 4.7 model, and asked, "How can we make Empire better?" Santi: Not entirely, but I did do it. I exported data from Megaphone and YouTube, went through all the shows since 2021, and ran correlation analyses with crypto asset prices. We often say "views are linked to crypto prices," but the actual data shows a lower correlation. Jason: So, not that correlated? Santi: Yes. Crypto content generally falls into two categories: one is purely about price, which goes viral on YouTube; our in-depth interview-style content performs better on Spotify and Apple, but not on YouTube. Retail investors on YouTube gain followers fastest during bull markets; they follow the market trends. Jason: YouTube's algorithm will penalize us. TLDR means we need to create more sensational headlines, but that's not our brand image. I've talked to Brian and David from Bankless many times about this; their thumbnails are very exaggerated. But I'm too proud; there's a line I'll never cross: I won't create content like BitBoy. Santi: Respect their path. Jason: Never become BitBoy, and never become Joe McCann (a prominent figure in the crypto space). Okay, let's get to the point. Market Overview: The Two Sides of the S&P 500's 10-Day Rally and the AI ​​Narrative Santi: Wait, let me talk about the market first. Two weeks ago we were discussing a 10% market downturn, and now the Nasdaq has just completed a 10-day winning streak. Jason: Yes, that's historically very rare. I looked up the data, and a 10-day winning streak only happens about once every 5 to 7 years on average. Since the dot-com bubble in 2000, it's only happened a few times. Santi: To be honest, the past 10 trading days have been one of the most unusual ten days since 1950. The S&P 500 rose 9.8%, placing it at the 99.7th percentile of all historical 10-day gains. Jason: I've been thinking about this today: Just stay optimistic. If you're optimistic, you're in the right place. I don't know how this round will end, whether it will end, or if this is a new paradigm, but the data is there and cannot be ignored. Last week, we saw a great in-depth report on Hyperliquid in Colossus (an interview magazine founded by Patrick O'Shaughnessy). On a macro level, I'm willing to change my mind at any time. I recently listened to a 4-hour podcast by Leopold Aschenbrenner (former OpenAI researcher, now managing a hedge fund). This guy was right when he was fired from OpenAI two years ago. For the first hour or two of the podcast, he was discussing how we'll reach AGI sooner than most people expect, and if it falls into the hands of different regimes, it's a nuclear arms race-level risk. He advocates for the nationalization of AI labs and opposes open source. You could hear the tension in his tone. He felt that very few people truly understood how fast and far this could go, and the market wasn't taking it seriously enough. Santi: Was that the "Situational Awareness" article he wrote two years ago? Jason: Yes. I tweeted, "I finally finished reading Leopold's article on AI. You should read it whether you're interested in AI or not. These are my notes." That tweet went viral, with over 10,000 saves and 1.6 million views. But my mistake was only taking notes; I didn't buy anything. Santi: He bought some himself. How big is his fund now? Jason: I don't know the initial size, but it's $5.5 billion now. He changes his portfolio frequently. His largest holdings now include Bloom Energy (a hydrogen energy company), where he invested $300 million, now worth $1 billion; and a large position in SanDisk (memory chips). By the way, his partner is Anthropic CEO Dario's Chief of Staff. Santi: That's quite a bit of information. Before working at a hedge fund and joining OpenAI, he also worked at the Future Fund, a project funded by Sam Bankman-Fried of FTX. In that podcast episode, he talked about his experience working with SBF and the lessons he learned, which was quite interesting. Jason: Here's some data about FTX: SBF held 7.8% of Anthropic at the time. Did you see the news this week? Anthropic's new valuation is $800 billion. FTX's stake would be worth more than the entire market capitalization of Coinbase. Santi: Yes, and it ended up being liquidated by the custodian, for about $1.5 billion. Jason: Crazy. FTX creditors ultimately received their full payments, even with a premium. Public market investors are very nervous now, always looking for something. There's both fear and excitement surrounding AI, a coin with two sides. This sentiment brings dramatic volatility and pullbacks, but the market fundamentally wants to go up. Everyone realizes this is an out-of-control train, unstoppable. Santi: On one hand, no one knows how to model the positive and negative impacts of AI into valuations. These labs are releasing one groundbreaking technology after another, and not being able to keep up creates tension. On the other hand, after listening to Leopold, Elon, and Dario's podcasts, you get a sense of awe that "we are at the tipping point of human civilization." Rockets fly into outer space and come back; the market is both nervous and willing to move upwards. Trading Philosophy: Finding Alpha Through Consumption Habits Jason: Could you elaborate on your position? Santi: I tweeted about Situational Awareness, and haven't sold anything since. I'm just a bag holder; I buy and hold. I'm not a very interesting podcast guest because I don't trade much. I took Robinhood from $10 to $15 and all the way to $125, and I'm still adding to my position. DoorDash is the same; the company I spend the most money on year after year is the one I keep adding to. Jason: That's really clever logic. I always remember what you said before: look at your annual spending patterns; there are some companies you spend more and more on each year. Amazon is one, and DoorDash is another. Santi: I took a class in my freshman year where a friend's father was a guest speaker. He was managing a hedge fund of about $5 billion to $10 billion at the time. He said all the best trades of his career came from observing his and his children's spending. His daughter said, "I want an iPhone," so he bought an Apple product; his daughter said, "My friends are all using Snapchat," so he participated in Snapchat's early funding round. He told us 18- and 19-year-olds: You don't realize it, being close to the front lines is alpha. The older you get, the harder it is to acquire alpha. He also said: We are moving towards a world without alpha on the internet; you have to see it with your own eyes and use it yourself to discover it. Jason: Reddit used to be full of alpha. There was a hedge fund that made money by scraping Reddit. Before the release of the Barbie movie, he read Reddit reviews and found that "this movie is a masterpiece," while Rotten Tomatoes gave it a low score. He then heavily invested in Mattel (the manufacturer of Barbie) and made a fortune. So you can create a Claude instance and have it scrape all sorts of information from Reddit.

Hyperliquid In-Depth Analysis: Wall Street's New Darling

Santi: Okay, let's get to the news.

Jason: There's so much to talk about: Tether's endorsement of Drift's (a perpetual contract protocol on Solana) user relief plan; Tether launching a new wallet—these are two news items about Tether; Rob's VC manifesto; Kraken raising $200 million and preparing for an IPO; and Hyperliquid's in-depth coverage on Colossus. Let's start with Hyperliquid.

Santi: Let me first introduce Colossus. Patrick O'Shaughnessy runs the Invest Like the Best podcast and Positive Sum asset management company. He launched a new print and digital magazine called Colossus, which specializes in in-depth feature articles on tech finance figures, and the quality is top-notch globally.

This time, they wrote about the story of Hyperliquid founder Jeff Yan. Jason: Let me steal a point from you. You said something very apt in our episode with Logan Jastremski: it's been a long time since I've felt this much interest in a crypto project from outside the crypto space. Just like Polymarket's explosive growth due to the presidential election controversy, Jeff has now captured Wall Street's imagination. The geopolitical catalyst this time is everyone wanting to trade commodities over the weekend, and Hyperliquid caught that wave of traffic. Santi: I think even if the situation between Iran and the Strait of Hormuz eases, this trend won't stop. The train has already started moving; you'll see more trading volume and activity pouring in. Currently, the trading volume of non-crypto assets on Hyperliquid has already surpassed that of crypto assets. Jason: Really? Santi: Commodities are now Hyperliquid's second-largest asset class, second only to Bitcoin. I'll make a predictable one: commodities will surpass Bitcoin this year. Hyperliquid could become one of the top five crypto companies by market capitalization. It's currently $25 billion, and I think there's a clear path to $100 billion. Coinbase and Robinhood are in that category. If Hyperliquid continues to execute well, they have the technical talent, they've already won over crypto traders, and now they're winning over Wall Street traders. Jason: Here are the numbers. Bitcoin accounts for 25% of Hyperliquid's holdings, commodities 17%, Ethereum 16%, HYPE tokens themselves 10%, index tokens (likely leveraged S&P 500) 7%, L1 tokens 4%, stocks 4%, Solana 3.5%, followed by meme coins, privacy coins, and DeFi. Santi: I think the most interesting story in the crypto world right now is probably the growth of the oil market on Hyperliquid. This is thanks to Trade XYZ (the derivatives front-end on the Hyperliquid ecosystem) created by the Shoku team. When the situation with Iran broke out, trading volume and open interest exploded because the oil market was closed for the weekend, and Hyperliquid became the only pricing venue over the weekend. This thing crossed the mainstream divide. Jason: We specifically built a website called Weekend Markets (weekendmarkets.com) to display all this data. When traditional markets are closed, Trade XYZ is the only active price discovery venue. Weekend trading volume is in the billions of dollars, and the median error in predicting Monday's opening price is only 50 basis points. Now, Trade XYZ prices are the most informative signal in the entire financial market relative to Friday's closing price. The website shows all traded stocks and commodities, charts, heatmaps, and methodologies. Traditional institutional investors are now likely more interested in Hyperliquid's weekend trading than anything else in crypto. Santi: Oil daily trading volume is $500 million, with open interest at $350 million. Jason: People outside the crypto world often ask you questions. Are they primarily interested in commodities or stocks? Santi: The most interesting thing is Hyperliquid itself. I'd say this is the biggest interest traditional hedge fund managers have in crypto since Paul Tudor Jones called Bitcoin "the fastest horse" in May 2020—in six years. This time, the interest is in Hyperliquid, with weekend trading being the core driver. I received a call this week from a hedge fund manager asking me to explain Hyperliquid to him. His fund is very large, and he and his team spend most of their time on Twitter, getting their trading inspiration from Twitter as well. This incident perfectly illustrates the importance of Twitter. He said a top trader he knew tweeted about Hyperliquid, and he followed up by listening to every podcast Jeff appeared on. Their fund couldn't directly buy HYPE tokens, so they bought Hyperliquid's DAT (Digital Asset Treasury), which happened to be DAT's bull case. Now it's one of their largest positions, and he said it's one of the few assets he could buy that isn't related to AI trading. Jason: I just had AI explain Hyperliquid to TradFi: "Imagine CME moving its matching engine, order book, clearinghouse, and margin system all onto an integrated blockchain, with every transaction, clearing, and funding fee recorded in an immutable public ledger. That's Hyperliquid." Hyperliquid Compliance Prospects: CME is the Best Benchmark. Santi: Is it possible that once Hyperliquid is approved for compliance in the US, it will undergo a large-scale revaluation? Jason: I'm neither a lawyer nor a regulator, so this is beyond my expertise. But Hyperliquid did hire Jake Chervinsky (former Chief Legal Officer of Variant Fund) to head the Hyperliquid Policy Institute. The logic is this: Following the 2008 financial crisis, the Dodd-Frank Act required futures trading through regulated entities like CME and Cboe, aiming for transparency and making all transactions visible to prevent black swan events. If you were Hyperliquid or Jake, your argument to the CFTC would be: you created this rule precisely for transparency, to prevent further problems. We have DeFi, where all transactions are completely transparent, and the clearing mechanism largely passed the stress test on October 10th. If they haven't argued this way, they should. Imagine Hyperliquid being approved in the US; it would instantly revalue. CME currently has a market capitalization of $117 billion; the market always needs a benchmark. Santi: I'd give it about a 30% to 40% probability. Jason: Have you talked to hedge funds and heard any negative opinions about Hyperliquid? Santi: Not much. Most people are just starting to understand and haven't formed a clear bullish or bearish view yet. Don't forget that Solana's peak protocol revenue also reached $100 million, mostly from meme coin trading. Jason: The peak of REV (Real Economic Value). Santi: Yes, in January 2024 or 2025, Solana reached $500 million in REV, almost all of it in meme coins. Now the question is: should the market give the protocol revenue generated by meme coin trading the same valuation multiple as the protocol revenue generated by TradFi commodity trading? Jason: Of course not, commodity revenue is of higher quality. Santi: So I think Hyperliquid can achieve a much larger scale than that. Jason: I saw some data: the protocols most mentioned by all speakers at DAS (Digital Asset Summit, a summit hosted by Blockworks). We crawled all the panels, fireside sessions, and keynotes. Bitcoin was mentioned 742 times, and Ethereum 143 times—those two are definitely outliers. Third is Hyperliquid with 66 times, Morpho (a lending protocol) with 59 times, followed by Solana, Canton, Espresso (a shared ordering protocol), Circle, Stellar, Avalanche, Jupiter, Tether, Maple, Optimism, and Superstate. Espresso ranking 7th is quite unexpected. Drift's bailout plan and Tether's "killing two birds with one stone" Jason: What other news did you follow this week? Santi: Tether invested in Drift to help protocol users recover their losses (Drift suffered a vulnerability attack earlier this year that affected approximately $285 million). This approach is similar to Bitfinex's strategy of issuing tokens to compensate victims, with affected users gradually recovering their losses through future transaction fees. This time, Tether is providing up to approximately $127 million in support, bringing the total to nearly $150 million. Jason: The specific mechanism requires users to trade with USDT on Drift to recover their losses. This is a win-win for Tether, as it both does public relations and locks in the use of stablecoins. Many people on Twitter are comparing it to USDC; reportedly, someone contacted USDC to freeze hacker funds, but Circle's response was not as quick as Tether's. This is a loss for Circle. Santi: This is a clever mechanism that incentivizes users to continue using the protocol. To some extent, this is the only viable solution; another option is to dilute the protocol through large-scale issuance at the underlying level.

SEC's Major Guidance and Clarity Bill Progress

Jason: There's also new guidance from the SEC. They stated this week that self-custodied DeFi front-ends are not subject to broker-dealer registration rules. Previously, a major attack on DeFi during the Gensler era required these protocols to register as broker-dealers, which fundamentally contradicts the permissionless nature of DeFi and is practically impossible. On Tuesday, the SEC's Division of Trading and Markets issued a staff statement excluding crypto wallet interfaces from broker-dealer registration. The SEC explicitly told MetaMask, Phantom, and virtually all DeFi front-ends: you are not brokers. This ends one of the biggest legal mysteries surrounding DeFi. They created a new category called "covered user interface": if you help users translate transaction parameters into on-chain instructions, you belong to this category. Custodied wallets are excluded, but self-custodied wallets, browser plugins, mobile applications, and software embedded with self-custodied wallets are protected.

Santi: I'm surprised DeFi tokens haven't been revalued as a result. Jason: Robinhood was the one that revalued due to regulatory benefits; they lowered the minimum $25,000 equity requirement for day traders. Overall, Aave rose 20% this week, and Bitcoin hit $75,000. I think this is mainly due to the increased probability of the Clarity Act (crypto market structure legislation) passing. Last week you asked Empire listeners if Clarity would pass, and everyone said 50/50. I was in Washington, D.C. last week, and everyone said 50/50. Now it's probably around 60/40 or even 65/35. A few crypto celebrities went on CNBC and said, "If Clarity doesn't pass before April 1st, we're completely dead," and for some reason, that statement has been accepted. But from what I understand, even if it's delayed until May 1st, or even August, or 2027, there's still a chance. Of course, the longer it drags on, the more garbage clauses will be crammed into the bill. Some say that if it's delayed until mid-2027, it will become the "DeFi Act" (meaning it will be heavily modified by interest groups). MicroStrategy's increased holdings and the 11.5% return on STRC. Santi: I tend to think that the good performance of crypto is due to a good macro environment, and the correlation is very strong. Regarding Bitcoin, MicroStrategy raised money this week through STRC (Perpetual Preferred Stock) to buy nearly 14,000 Bitcoins. Jason: Total holdings are 780,000 Bitcoins, total investment is close to $60 billion, and the average cost is $75,000. This is one of their largest acquisitions. Now, besides looking at mNAV premiums or discounts, the market is also watching STRC's dynamics. Santi: There's a protocol called Saturn whose TVL (Total Value Locked) is growing rapidly. Essentially, it packages STRC onto the blockchain, offering an annualized return of approximately 11.5%. Jason: I went to MicroStrategy's office in Tysons Corner, Virginia on Thursday, and the entire meeting was about STRC. STRC's annualized return of 11.5% is very attractive given the generally low yields in on-chain DeFi. I won't discuss the risks today; I'll leave that for later. We should invite Saylor or someone from MicroStrategy to the show to talk about it. What everyone is concerned about is "where does this 11.5% return come from, its sustainability, and what are the risks?" Kraken's Valuation Halved and a Breakdown of the Exchange's Business Model Santi: Speaking of Kraken, they took $200 million from Deutsche Börse. Jason: The valuation dropped from $20 billion at the end of 2025 to $13.3 billion, which is a significant discount. Santi: I haven't seen that valuation figure. It's probably a secondary market transaction; they bought common stock instead of preferred stock, hence the structural difference. Jason: Yes, Deutsche Börse bought 1.5% of the shares at a valuation of $13.3 billion. My guess is that because it's common stock, there was a discount of around 35%. Additionally, Kraken experienced a small-scale security incident, unrelated to user funds, which was an internal issue within the support team. I'll mention it as a footnote. However, Kraken is a very well-run company with the potential to reach Coinbase's scale. Arjun (Arjun Sethi, Kraken's co-CEO) is doing a great job. I might look into secondary market opportunities. Santi: I invested in Backpack (an exchange founded by Armani Ferrante), which was much smaller but very interesting. Armani is an excellent builder. The core issue in evaluating an exchange's business model is the ratio of fixed to variable costs. Exchanges are essentially tied to crypto prices; they are very profitable when prices are high, but their profitability is compressed during downturns. I previously conducted a comparative analysis of Coinbase, Robinhood, and Kraken. Robinhood was the best, with diversified business (a high proportion of non-crypto trading volume) and the ability to significantly reduce costs during bear markets. Coinbase was second, and Kraken was third. But that was an analysis before Arjun took over. They later restructured the team, bringing in many capable operators, and the DeFi team is doing very well. So perhaps my previous assessment was too conservative. Any business needs to consider operating leverage and resilience; in a bear market, profit margins cannot be crushed—this is the perspective of a cross-cycle investor. Jason: What about X Stocks? I've seen some discussions about pre-IPO stocks on Twitter, such as Anthropic. Santi: The trading volume is quite small, not as dramatic as oil. Jason: I have data; currently, pre-IPO related stock trading is around $17 million, which is still very small. I spoke with the X Stocks team on Kraken a few days ago, and they, along with other exchanges, believe that X Stocks will have a major breakthrough this year. The X Stocks team and others at Kraken are betting on this direction. Here's a tweet from Nikita, the X (Twitter) product manager: "X has always been the best source of financial information for traders and investors, with billions of dollars flowing daily into timeline-based content. Today we're launching the cashtag feature on iPhones in the US and Canada, bringing real-time financial data. When you search for or post a cashtag or contract address, X automatically matches stocks or tokens, and clicking on a cashtag shows price movements and related posts—all without leaving X." This is Elon's everything-app vision in progress. Scroll: Controversy and the "Ice Block Effect" of the L2 Track Santi: How's the Ethereum ecosystem lately? Besides Tom Lee saying ETH will reach $62,000. Jason: I don't know, I'm not that in-depth. The ECC (Ethereum Community Conference) just ended; the atmosphere was so-so, but there were still some interesting projects discussed. Santi: Ether.fi migrated from Scroll to the Optimism mainnet, migrating over 70,000 cards. Jason: You're an investor in Scroll, so I don't know how much you can talk about. Scroll seems to have done something rather unethical. When Ether.fi wanted to migrate from Scroll to Optimism, they manually increased the on-chain gas fees. One of Ether.fi's founders tweeted, "This is even more outrageous than people think." Santi: For context: Ether.fi is crucial to Scroll; if Ether.fi were separated, Scroll would have very little TVL. I don't have firsthand information about what happened internally. The publicly available on-chain data shows that in the first six days of April, Scroll increased the fees for publishing data on the Ethereum mainnet by 1000 times, two orders of magnitude. Users ended up paying over $50,000 more in fees as a result. The timing is indeed suspicious, coinciding with the Ether.fi migration window. The on-chain data is there, and it looks pretty bad. Jason: What went wrong with Scroll? Santi: I was an early investor. At the time, ZK-rollups and zero-knowledge proofs were hot, and I thought this approach itself had value and meaning. Scroll's co-founder was a deep cryptographer, so I bet on it. But it wasn't just Scroll; many projects in the entire L2 space faded into obscurity. Can you name a single L2 project that performed well? Vitalik's article about the L2 roadmap earlier this year was itself an admission; the market had already noticed this. The price performance of Optimism, Arbitrum, ZKsync, etc., all illustrate this point. Scroll is currently valued at $8 million, with a fully diluted valuation of $45 million, lower than its initial funding round valuation. That's a terrible situation. Jason: Can it come back? What does it need? Where will the trading volume come from? Santi: This reminds me of something I've talked about with many infrastructure providers: they are melting ice, whether they want to admit it or not. In 2017, without users and data, you could sell your dreams. Any founder, Elon or Scroll, had to sell their dreams before cashing in. The crypto world has long been in a "suspension period" of white papers and experiments, and the market has been patient with that. Not today. A year ago, I said there would be a serious "property owners vs. propertyless" divide in the crypto world, and today that divide is already very large; you can see this by comparing Hyperliquid's activity level with other projects. Three Investment Themes and the New Cycle of "Protocol True Zeroing" Santi: The neobank category is quite popular recently. Plasma (a dedicated chain for stablecoins), Ether.fi are all working on it, and Aave also has its own neobank attempt. As an investor, I have three main themes, which are also reflected in my personal holdings: 1. The demand for stablecoins outside the US will continue to expand and is far from being met; 2. The world will only become more volatile; 3. There is a serious global affordability crisis. These three pillars all have on-chain implementation paths, and the investment strategy should follow this line of thinking. However, most other projects face difficulties. Solana's REV also plummeted from a peak of $500 million to $20 million. What can revive Solana? There are some positive aspects at the core: Western Union chose Solana as a pilot, and the team and engineers are very capable, genuinely thinking about the problem. However, in my conversations with these teams, I feel they must go for the jugular, using capital or unconventional methods to cement themselves as a persistent protocol. Finally, I want to say that in this round, I believe some protocols will truly go to zero, not just settle into dust for the next round. The old approach was "hold dust, dust will become the moon," like Lend merging into Aave. This round won't be so widespread. The market prefers to concentrate funds in Hyperliquid and Bitcoin. The three major narratives of the next round and RWA trends. Jason: Besides Hyperliquid and Bitcoin, what else might ordinary people be interested in or have heard of when you talk to them? Santi: Stablecoins and prediction markets are hot topics, but prediction markets currently don't have tokens. Jason: These things take time to gain widespread public awareness. We've always said that last year's three hot topics were perpetual contracts, prediction markets, and stablecoins. Projects doing these three things were able to secure funding and users, while projects outside these areas were crushed. This year, there will be three new things, but I don't know what they are. Santi: Will it? Jason: I guess it will. The term tokenization (stocks on-chain, RWA real-world assets) has been overused, but it will be one of the three hot topics this year. I met Daniel (founder of Tokeny) a few days ago; the ERC-3643 standard he spearheaded is becoming the mainstream RWA standard. RWA is a key focus this year. I said the show should talk more about what traditional issuers are doing and will do on-chain. I think these activities will happen on public permissionless networks. Santi: Jamie Dimon's (JPMorgan CEO) shift in stance is quite interesting, from outright opposition to crypto to now clearly leaning towards "blockchain is something we can do." The gap between his stance and Larry Fink's (BlackRock CEO) has never been smaller, and it's narrowing every month. He's been a fierce opponent of the Clarity Act, and has publicly attacked Brian Armstrong (Coinbase CEO). I think TradFi is stalling for time before Clarity passes, trying to ensure their views are reflected in the legislation. Jason: Jamie Dimon said on CBS that they're considering "prediction market services." Santi: What are prediction market services? They just want to come in and take a commission. But everyone wants to do prediction markets. To me, the prediction market is just another form of derivatives and CDS (Credit Default Swap), and the boundaries are becoming increasingly blurred.

YouTube Comments and Closing

Jason: The YouTube comments section is here. I haven't read it in a while. Should we hear the bad reviews first or the good ones?

Santi: Let's hear the bad reviews first.

Jason: Comment 1: "I like Santi. The Santi who claims to be a full-time crypto investor but completely missed out on Hyperliquid. The Inversion project he invested in went to zero before it even launched." Comment 2: "I like Santi, but he doesn't seem to know anything about DeFi. He hasn't done any airdrops in years. What makes him qualified to talk about these things?"

Santi: I never said I was qualified.

Jason: Comment 3: "Santi is a jack-of-all-trades, master of none. He's good at telling us what's already happened, but not good at predicting the future." Santi: Well said. Jason: Comment 4: "Just want you to know Empire is my favorite crypto podcast. I live in Bangkok so I can't come to DAS." That's a valid reason. All the negative reviews are directed at you; nobody's criticizing me. Santi: People are even discussing how handsome you are and how good your opinions are. Rob (Rob Hadick, Dragonfly partner) even had the entire Dragonfly team helping you remove negative reviews. Jason: This week's recommended content? Santi: I just listened to 2.5 hours of Leopold Aschenbrenner's 4-hour podcast. There's a podcast about Amazon VP Ethan Evans that everyone recommended; I'm planning to listen to it. Jason: I'm going back to listen to David Senra's Founders podcast and his founder interviews. I've talked about the DoorDash Tony Xu episode many times. I just finished listening to the Evan Spiegel (Snap founder) episode; it was alright. Some people are born with the "it factor" for storytelling, some aren't. Tony Xu has it, Evan has it to a certain extent. My "it" recommendation this week is Allbirds (a shoe company that went bankrupt and was sold). They made an AI, allbirds.ai, which is a ChatGPT shell; you ask it any question and it answers "shoes shoes shoes." The stock rose tenfold yesterday. Santi: When something like this happens, we can be sure we're closer to the top than the bottom. Jason: My weekly routine is watching Seinfeld for the first time. After a day's work, I only have 20 minutes to shut down my brain. We've been working on a few things that haven't been made public yet, and the pace is fast; I often can't even finish an episode in 20 minutes. Santi: How culturally disconnected am I? I haven't watched a single episode of Seinfeld. I didn't watch TV at all when I was a kid. Jason: Do you have a TV in your room now? Santi: No. I don't keep my phone in the bedroom either. Jason: I don't have one in my bedroom either. When we bought this house, the previous owner had a big TV on the wall, and I said, "No TV in the bedroom," which was a mistake; I should have kept it. To wrap things up: We're going to make Empire even better. Next, we'll be talking more about publicly traded companies doing interesting crypto-related businesses, including Better (a mortgage lending company), Figure, Klarna, and Western Union. Besides Hyperliquid, this could be the biggest catalyst for the next cycle, with traditional companies starting to implement it. I've been talking to the Framework team recently about Better's positioning, and we're also researching Figure; there are some interesting things going on. Santi: We also spoke with the Figure team a few days ago; it's really interesting. Jason: Okay, that's all for today. Thanks for listening, and keep criticizing us in the comments and on Telegram. See you next week. Santi: Have a great weekend.

data.title: Bitwise Advisor: From the K-shaped economy to AI taking away jobs, how can BTC save young people?
data.description:
Source: "When Shift Happens" podcast; Compiled by: Felix, PANews

Jeff Park, a Bitwise advisor and macro strategist, was formerly the Chief Investment Officer of ProCap Financial. Recently, in a podcast, he analyzed why real estate is actually a depreciating asset, why Bitcoin is the ultimate safe haven, and how AI will trigger a wave of Bitcoin adoption. Jeff Park believes that for young people, from unaffordable housing to AI replacing an entire generation's jobs, the financial system has completely collapsed.

PANews has compiled the highlights of the conversation.

Host: You told me before that you were exposed to the concept of "currency devaluation" very early on.

... Could you elaborate on this? Jeff Park: Absolutely. I grew up in both the US and South Korea. I spent elementary school in South Korea, right in the middle of the 1997 Asian financial crisis. That crisis left a deep impression on me, even though I was only in second or third grade at the time, because I witnessed the absolute unity a nation could display when it couldn't control its own destiny. It's a strange feeling when you find people everywhere, from upstairs to downstairs, bound together by a patriotism directly linked to the value of their sovereign currency. Perhaps the most relatable similar event for Americans is 9/11, which united people from all walks of life, regardless of their political leanings, to reflect on "what America is." In South Korea, the widespread devaluation of the currency also served this purpose of national unity. I clearly remember the South Korean government calling on citizens to donate gold to restore the national treasury and repay the IMF (International Monetary Fund) bailout loans, because those loans had extremely harsh terms. In emerging markets like South Korea, the IMF is perceived as having a strong political dimension, which may have contributed to my involvement in the crypto space 20 years later. Host: You just mentioned that the currency devaluation you experienced when you were young brought people together. But that was in Asia, now it's in the United States. What's happening in the US today? Can people really come together? Jeff Park: I think America's greatest strength is also its greatest weakness, which is its diversity. Commentators in Asia often predict that "demographic diversity will destroy America." In South Korea, achieving national cohesion is easy because everyone is Korean, bound together by a shared history of resisting colonial oppression. But in the US, it's hard to find an obvious bond that inspires a spirit of sacrifice throughout society. Sacrifice is the key word. For example, in South Korea, all men, regardless of class or education, are required to serve in the military, creating homogenized social norms among men. But in the US, it's hard to find a typical "American experience" that brings people together. Politics often draws lines between left and right, upper and lower classes, young and old, but I think these are just distractions. What's truly lacking and what needs to be cherished is a sense of national identity and solidarity among the younger generation. Host: You entered the workforce just as the 2008 financial crisis hit, followed by years of accelerated money printing. Now we live in New York, the world's financial center, where everything is ridiculously expensive. I'm Swiss, now living in Singapore, and I'm used to high prices, but it's still unbelievable here. How do ordinary people survive? People have clearly felt the inflation these past few years; what exactly happened? Jeff Park: Yes, what we're seeing is a completely out-of-control, utterly collapsed financial system. The lower classes are experiencing a "K-shaped economy." A "K-shaped economy" means that one group is enjoying the economic prosperity brought about by asset inflation, while another group is experiencing recession. They can't find jobs, and the wealth gap is widening, hence the K-shape—a dual-track economy. In New York, you can clearly see this through the asset class of real estate. Over the past 10 years, the average home price in New York City hasn't actually increased; it's remained flat. Those "super mansions" that serve as stores of value are selling extremely well. They're not for living in; they're assets that the wealthy buy to preserve value on their balance sheets. If you bought a $20 million penthouse seven years ago, you might sell it for $30 million now. But if you bought a regular home intended for a real life, to support your family, and to make a real economic contribution to the city, the price has actually decreased or remained flat. For example, New York has a "mansion tax" levied on properties over $1 million. Decades ago, $1 million could indeed buy a mansion, but now in New York, $1 million only buys a studio apartment. The government deliberately doesn't link this tax to inflation so it can collect more tax revenue. New York is a paradox, and all of this is a symptom of a lack of quality assets that preserve value.

Host: Why has the real estate market become like this?

Jeff Park: Land is inherently scarce.

The United States enjoys absolute privilege in operating the global financial system, and the dollar is its largest export product. However, this comes at a cost; offshore funds must ultimately flow back and be invested in US assets to maintain the trade deficit. This creates an artificial, bubble-like market for US assets, where overseas investors simply need a place to park their money. This raises a serious problem: the pricing motives of this market have nothing to do with those of us who actually live in New York and want to settle down. Host: For someone aged 30 to 35 who has saved some money, how should they invest? I think $1 million is a lot of money, but in New York, it can only buy an apartment, while you're telling me that you have to buy a $20 million penthouse to make a good investment. How should our generation view the "buy a house for investment" mentality of the previous generation? Jeff Park: The rise in property prices isn't because the physical value of the houses themselves has increased, but because the dollar is constantly depreciating. If you think about it carefully, real estate is a capital expenditure that requires constant maintenance. Stones weather, and you need to pay property taxes, mortgage taxes, maintenance fees, and homeowner's insurance. Houses are essentially depreciating assets; in fact, US tax law allows real estate investors to write off depreciation within 20 to 30 years. People use homeownership as a primary form of savings because it's inextricably linked to social functions—for example, paying hefty property taxes to secure a place in a public school for your child. Currently, the real estate market faces two major problems: liquidity transformation and demographic shifts. Did you know that the average age of Americans applying for mortgages today is 59? This is clearly not first-time homebuyers, but rather people in their sixties buying their third or fourth home. This directly squeezes out young people in their mid-twenties who want to buy their first home and start a family, essentially blocking their path to family life. Furthermore, when New Yorkers move to Austin, Texas to buy homes because of high taxes, Austin locals are also angry because their home prices have been inflated by New York capital. This is a problem of capital control; young people are being completely squeezed out of the market. Host: As a rational man in my 30s, I have a job, a girlfriend, and plan to get married and have children in the future, so I need a house. But you're telling me that buying a house is even a bad investment. I only have $100,000 or $500,000 in savings, what should I do? Jeff Park: To be honest, in a core city like New York, renting is definitely a more economical choice. When you own a house, various taxes, property fees, and insurance will eat up your return on capital to less than 2% or even less than 1%. You'd be better off putting your money in a money market fund and earning a risk-free 3.5%. Buying a house is purely gambling on rising property prices. Therefore, for young people without children, renting is the most economically correct outcome. But once you have children, you need stability, you need to send your children to school, and you have to pay a high "premium" for that peace of mind. This is no longer just a simple economic calculation. This is why modern young people are reluctant to have children; once they have children, they can no longer continue renting, disrupting their entire lifecycle and creating immense pressure. In Asia, such as Japan and South Korea, another common phenomenon is that young people are anxiously waiting for the wealth of their parents to be transferred after their parents pass away. However, the lifespan of older generations is increasing, and this time lag creates significant social friction between the two generations. Host: Are we doomed to wait desperately until we're 60, hoping our parents will leave us some property? Are there any other options? Jeff Park: Yes. There is now a better way to preserve wealth than real estate. This wealth requires no service, takes up no space, requires no maintenance, and isn't taxed every time—that's Bitcoin. Bitcoin will directly alleviate pressure on the real estate market. The billionaire who went to New York to buy a $40 million penthouse to transfer $50 million can now directly buy Bitcoin. You don't need to pay huge annual maintenance fees, nor do you need to worry about the government arbitrarily confiscating your property using "expropriation rights." Once this hot money, meant for preserving value, stops flowing into real estate, the demand curve will reset, housing prices will fall, and young people will be able to afford homes. Although there will be short-term pain from falling real estate prices, it's a win-win situation for society as a whole. This is why Michael Saylor calls Bitcoin "digital real estate," just like Manhattan land 100 years ago. Capital naturally gravitates towards efficient places; if you don't give it an outlet, society will eventually collapse. Host: You mentioned "smart investors" in your article. What are "smart investors"? Why have they declined? Jeff Park: "Smart investors" refers to value investors like Buffett or Benjamin Graham, who look for stocks that are extremely cheap relative to cash flow and have low price-to-earnings ratios. But I think that era is over. Because the best-performing assets now aren't "cheap" things, but things that are "scarce" and people believe have additional value. The entire framework of the "smart investor" is built on the assumption that everything must be priced according to the "risk-free rate" (i.e., US Treasury bonds). However, due to challenges to the creditworthiness of the US government, the foundation of the risk-free rate has been shaken. This is why the traditional 60/40 (stock/bond) portfolio has failed, and why the correlation between US Treasuries and US stocks is increasing. Once you remove the risk-free rate as a valuation anchor, the market becomes a chaotic mess. Host: So what is an "ideological investor"? Jeff Park: Traditional value investors try to hedge against the influence of geopolitics, AI, and culture, seeking so-called intrinsic value. "Ideological investors," on the other hand, take a different approach. They spend a lot of time predicting the future, focusing on capital flows and paradigm shifts in liquidity. They understand that the US government is actively buying assets, so they buy what the "White House asset management company" would buy. They know how to identify asset manipulation and avoid traditional valuation traps. Host: This sounds like the job of a Wall Street Chief Investment Officer (CIO). Can you explain it in simpler terms that older women can understand? Jeff Park: Actually, older women are very good at this. They know that the truly valuable things aren't Apple stock in their securities accounts. The most valuable things might exist in the physical realm, like her unique jewelry, or the Hermès bags in her wardrobe (which have outperformed the S&P 500 for 20 years). Or some great works of art. These things, traditionally not considered financial assets, are the real tools for wealth diversification. Your financial advisor will only teach you to buy a 60/40 stock/bond portfolio, private equity, or venture capital, but it's essentially the same thing; they're all subject to the same "global arbitrage trade" and risk-free interest rates. What you need is a different pool of assets, things that macroeconomic cycles never touch—that's true decorrelated diversification. Cryptocurrencies, gold, Hermès bags, limited-edition sneakers, and Pokémon cards all fall into this category. I also believe that a major asset class of the future is "data." Young people have realized that Facebook is profiting from their data, and in the future, they will control and monetize their data themselves through decentralized technologies such as prediction markets. Wall Street advisors will never teach you how to play prediction markets, but this will be the trend because young people know that traditional financial games are manipulated, and they crave alternatives. This is why Bitcoin, DeFi, and sports betting have emerged.

Host: Macroeconomist Raoul Pal said, "Diversification is dead," and that the performance of all assets is only related to money printing and fiat currency devaluation, so he's all in cryptocurrencies. What do you think?

Jeff Park: I both agree and disagree. If you're only focusing on traditional assets manipulated by the same set of global liquidity, then diversification is indeed meaningless. But if you broaden your perspective and focus on investment categories that are not manipulated by this flow of funds, diversification still has value.

Last year, I proposed the "Aggressive Portfolio Theory," listing 25 different uncorrelated assets. For example, gold, in Asian culture, remains the most primitive and irreplaceable store of value. Great works of art; during the 2008 financial crisis, the best trades were actually in the art asset class. There are also those trading scarce, high-end wines. I'm very optimistic about "tokenization" in the cryptocurrency space, but my initial intention wasn't to tokenize BlackRock's funds, but rather to tokenize high-barrier-to-entry long-tail alternative assets like wine and superyachts. This way, even without millions, ordinary people can buy a small portion of this yacht for $100, achieving a risk-averse portfolio like that of billionaires. Host: But this is still too complicated for ordinary people. For example, my 35-year-old sister is just an ordinary office worker; how can she accumulate wealth? Jeff Park: Over the past 20 years, young people have become more financially savvy. Don't laugh when you see young people trading limited-edition sneakers and Pokémon trading cards. This is precisely the diversified wealth thinking that young people need, instead of blindly chasing Nvidia stock. Young people are playing their own game, and if they succeed in it, that's incredibly powerful. Host: Now there's something else that's causing people to lose their jobs: AI. You wrote an article called "Occupy AI." Could you explain what "Occupy Wall Street" is before talking about "Occupy AI"? Jeff Park: There was the "Occupy Wall Street" movement in 2008, where angry people camped out in downtown New York City demanding justice. During the subprime mortgage crisis, banks were morally and legally at fault, privatizing profits while socializing losses (getting taxpayers to bail them out) without facing consequences. I believe AI will trigger a potentially even more extreme class struggle. We've never encountered a technology as disruptive as AI, capable of completely replacing labor while generating record profits for corporate executives. We will see a more extreme K-shaped economy, where corporate profits rise not because of increased revenue, but because of cost-cutting through layoffs. As I wrote in my article, "Amazon laid off 30,000 people while hitting record highs in the stock market, which illustrates the collapse of the price of free will and the soaring value of self-determination." Host: Can you explain what this means? Jeff Park: People work not just to earn money, but also to be productive, contribute to society, and set an example for their children. If people don't create value, they will have psychological problems. Past technological advancements (such as electricity, cars, trains, and email) have amplified human capabilities, allowing you to work faster and better, but the key is that you are still working. But some aspects of AI completely eliminate work. Even more disturbing is that, to support the construction of AI data centers, some are calling for government backing, packaging it as a survival crisis of "if we don't do it, other countries will." So governments use funds to invest in technologies that will precisely replace the jobs of their taxpayers. Do you think the public will support this self-funded, self-destructive plan? That's why an "Occupy AI" is inevitable. Unlike the explicit enemies on Wall Street dressed in suits and Hermès ties, AI is invisible. Tech giants will only say, "We're just a platform." Today's young people graduate with huge student loans, can't find jobs, can't afford houses, and may never have jobs again. Meanwhile, tech giants are each other's clients, funds are flowing between Microsoft, OpenAI, Anthropic, and Nvidia, creating new highs in the US stock market—this is absolutely abnormal. Host: You wrote at the end of your article, "Occupy Wall Street turned millennials into die-hard Bitcoin supporters, while Occupy AI will turn Generation Z and Alpha into Bitcoin supporters." Can you explain this in simple terms? Jeff Park: Everyone needs a moment of "awakening" or "enlightenment" to discover Bitcoin. For many millennials, this moment of epiphany came during the 2008 financial crisis and the COVID-19 pandemic, when we realized the existing monetary system was a scam. But for Generation Z and Alpha, currency devaluation no longer holds much appeal. They're already disillusioned and know the system is beyond saving. And with institutions like BlackRock buying up Bitcoin in large quantities, they even feel it's become "old people's game." But I believe AI will be the catalyst for their awakening. This generation will be competing with AI for jobs right out of school, which touches on their deepest personal interests. They'll find Bitcoin to be the best safe-haven asset. And if they feel AI is having a significant negative social impact, they'll vote with their feet. Both AI and Bitcoin are extremely energy-intensive, so they might choose to support Bitcoin. More importantly, the core of AI is centralization; it collects your data and uses it to replace you. If your data is used to train smarter models, you must be compensated. The only theoretical way to achieve this ownership traceability and value distribution is through decentralized encryption. This might inspire the younger generation to rediscover the "decentralized" nature of cryptocurrencies. I remain optimistic about AI benefiting society, but only if the issue of "data contribution compensation" is resolved. Host: Many people might feel that Bitcoin is currently fluctuating at $60,000, $70,000, or even hundreds of thousands of dollars, which is too expensive, and they've missed their last buying opportunity. What are your thoughts on this? Jeff Park: I think people need to consider: what downside risks do you face if you don't hold Bitcoin? If you don't hold Bitcoin, you're essentially shorting it. Fiat currencies are depreciating at an unprecedented rate. Looking back, when the dollar's fiscal deficit reached an uncontrollable escape velocity, you had to include the "fastest horse" in your portfolio—that is, Bitcoin—or assets like Hermès or Rolex that resist global arbitrage cycles. Host: As a CIO who emphasizes diversification, do you advise those who hold large amounts of Bitcoin in their portfolios as savings to adopt a defensive or offensive approach? Jeff Park: Many people in the industry adopt a "barbell strategy," holding half Bitcoin and half money market funds (cash), and nothing else. Personally, I prefer a more diversified asset allocation. But if I were forced to keep only two assets in my portfolio, my choices would be: first, Bitcoin, because it's the least correlated asset with global capital markets; second, you still need interest-bearing assets in the dollar system. For example, I believe we will eventually return to a zero-interest-rate environment (maintaining the global leverage game through interest rate cuts), so buying 30-year US Treasury bonds now is a good investment opportunity because bond prices rise when interest rates fall. This is essentially betting that America's innovative capabilities will eventually solve the problem. Host: How do you use the Bitcoin mindset to educate your two children to face the AI-dominated world of the future? Jeff Park: Bitcoin taught me one thing: always stay open and humble, because the world is bigger than any individual or model. It's a living experiment. A phrase I often tell my children is that it's not practice that creates perfection, but practice that creates progress. Bitcoin will never be absolutely perfect, nothing will ever be perfect, but we can continuously improve in the pursuit of our ideals.

data.title: Is the Fed Chair-elect really pro-crypto?
data.description:
Author: Thejaswini M A; Translator: BitpushNews

Before Kevin Warsh could head the Federal Reserve, he had to sell his holdings in Solana, dYdX, Optimism, Polymarket, Dapper Labs, and about twenty other companies. This is an odd prerequisite for a job, but it's a legal requirement and applies equally to anyone hoping to regulate the U.S. financial system, regardless of their understanding of it.

The disclosure that triggered all of this arrived on April 14. The 69-page document, submitted to the Office of Government Ethics, cleared the final administrative hurdle before his confirmation hearings.

The document is a standard compliance form. At the same time, if you know where to look, it's one of the most influential document records ever seen by Senate committees.

Walsh and his wife, Jane Lauder (whose family interests include Estée Lauder cosmetics, with a net worth estimated at around $1.9 billion by Forbes), hold combined assets worth at least $192 million. The majority of this is in positions held by two Juggernaut Fund LPs, each worth over $50 million, related to his advisory work for Stanley Druckenmiller's Duquesne Family Office. The underlying assets are protected by confidentiality agreements. Certification officials from the Government Ethics Office specifically flagged these assets and confirmed that Walsh will comply with federal ethics laws once he divests the assets he intends to hold. The portfolio encompasses decentralized finance (DeFi) protocols, Ethereum scaling networks, Bitcoin payment infrastructure, and prediction markets. It reads like he's scouted the entire crypto industry and deliberately bought a bit of everything in each category. Through AVGF I, Walsh indirectly holds stakes in Solana, Optimism, and the Lightning Network. Through DCM Investments 10 LLC, disclosures list dYdX, Polychain Capital, Compound, Blast, Lighter, and Lemon Cash. Another AVF fund series includes Dapper Labs, DeSo, Friends With Benefits, and Zero Gravity. He also directly holds shares in Web3 gaming company Metatheory, valued between $1,000 and $15,000. Flashnet, a Bitcoin merchant payments startup, is also included. Polymarket is also in the portfolio. These cryptocurrency positions are mostly within fund instruments, where individual projects are not reported in US dollars. According to the Government Office of Ethics, this means each item is valued at less than $1,000, representing small-scale venture capital investments rather than concentrated holdings. Size isn't the key factor; breadth is. This portfolio covers L1 blockchains, L2 scaling solutions, DeFi lending, decentralized derivatives, NFT infrastructure, Bitcoin payments, and prediction markets. The only missing categories are memes, game tokens, mining companies, and directly held Bitcoin. Everything he owns falls into the categories of infrastructure, financial pipelines, or developer tools. What does the money say? What Warsh's money represents is that cryptocurrency is the next layer of financial infrastructure, built on top of protocols that process real transactions, and what the people building these protocols are doing, in a way, very similar to fintech. The closest thing to a speculative bet in the entire disclosure is Friends With Benefits, a social token community. But considering all the other projects in the portfolio, it's more like someone invited him to dinner, and he politely wrote a small check. His past public statements align with this. In 2011, Warsh saw the Bitcoin white paper at a dinner hosted by Marc Andreessen. In 2018, he wrote in the Wall Street Journal that Bitcoin could become a sustainable store of value, similar to gold. In 2021, he stated on CNBC that Bitcoin is the "new gold" for people under 40. In 2025, at the Hoover Institution, he published his most comprehensive argument to date: Bitcoin is not a substitute for the dollar, but it can serve as an excellent "watchdog" of monetary policy. I wouldn't call Walsh a Bitcoin maximalist. A monetary policy professional who sees Bitcoin as a legitimate signal of the dollar's health might, in some ways, be more useful to the crypto industry.

Wash earned $10.2 million in consulting fees from the Duquesne Family Office, the investment arm of Stanley Druckenmiller, one of the world's most reputable macro investors, and one of the few who have seriously discussed the crypto space at this level.

It was Druckenmiller who told Morgan Stanley last month that stablecoins would become the payment system across the United States within 10 to 15 years. Then, in the same interview, he described the rest of the crypto space as "finding solutions to problems."

It was Druckenmiller who told Morgan Stanley last month that stablecoins would become the payment system across the United States within 10 to 15 years. Then, in the same interview, he said that the rest of the crypto space was about "finding solutions to problems."

... Warsh's crypto exposure flows through the same network as arguably the world's most reputable macro investor. Druckenmiller says stablecoins are the future and everything else is noise, and Warsh's portfolio is filled with the infrastructure that makes stablecoins work—this is starting to seem like a common argument. Warsh has pledged to divest all assets. Selling liquidity token positions is fairly straightforward. But unwinding LP stakes in Polychain Capital or venture capital fund positions in illiquid early-stage companies is much more difficult. Some funds with multiple LPs are not typically required to divest under Government Ethics Office (GEO) rules. But GEO certification officials specifically flagged Juggernaut Fund's positions and made full divestment a condition of compliance. The underlying assets in these positions remain undisclosed due to existing confidentiality agreements. Federal ethics rules typically require a one-year "cooling-off period" for matters that directly impact near-term financial interests. This could become crucial as the Federal Reserve weighs its options on stablecoin legislation, tokenized deposits and securities, and research into central bank digital currencies (CBDCs). Consider what this means in practice. Congress is currently debating stablecoin frameworks. The CLARITY Act is still progressing in the Senate. Banks are running pilot programs for tokenized deposits. The Federal Reserve plays a continuous role in all of this. And the person running the Fed—the one who has actually invested in DeFi protocols, Bitcoin payment infrastructure, and prediction markets—may sit on the sidelines for the first year, while his colleagues who have never used a crypto wallet are involved in decision-making. It would be incomplete to write about Warsh's cryptocurrency holdings without mentioning the context of his nomination. The Trump family's crypto businesses had already generated substantial returns before any crypto legislation was signed. According to the Wall Street Journal, World Liberty Financial—a DeFi project that lists Barron Trump as a "DeFi visionary" on its website—has already generated at least $1.2 billion in realized returns for the Trump family by early 2026. The executive order allowing 401(k) accounts to invest in cryptocurrencies was signed by a president whose family is simultaneously cashing out from crypto businesses. Warsh's holdings are small-scale venture investments through a fund structure. The Trump family's holdings are concentrated, large positions that directly benefit from federal policy. The two are different in size and structure. Their connection lies in this: a president who financially benefits from a cryptocurrency-friendly Federal Reserve has chosen a nominee for Fed chair who has a financial interest in the crypto industry he will be regulating. Whether this represents blatant corruption, strategic alignment, or simply a natural evolution of American institutions depends on perspective. And that perspective often depends on whether you happen to own the relevant assets yourself. Senator Thom Tillis has stated he will oppose any Fed nominee until the Justice Department's investigation into Powell reaches a fully transparent conclusion. The committee is partisan, meaning that confirmation could be delayed if even one Republican defectes. Jerome Powell's term as Federal Reserve Chairman will end on May 15. If Walsh is not confirmed by then, Powell will remain in a caretaker role, while oil prices are above $100, the situation in Iran remains unresolved, and the market is trying to determine whether the Fed will have a leader. For the crypto community, Walsh's asset disclosure is a classic "good news, bad news" joke. What's the good news? We finally have a Fed Chairman who can distinguish between a "liquidity pool" and a "swimming pool." What's the bad news? Because he knows "how sausages are made" (deep inside knowledge), federal ethics laws have effectively cornered him in a digital corner, leaving him to "hang out" for a full year—watching his colleagues, who "don't own a single coin," try to regulate a technology they probably think is a sequel to *The Matrix*. These two things can be true simultaneously. He understands DeFi from an internal perspective, and also believes that the Fed's primary responsibility is monetary stability, while asset price inflation is a symptom of policy failure. These views are destined to make future FOMC meetings very interesting. Warsh's disclosure document is less a signal of a systemic shift and more a quiet acknowledgment of path dependence. Our past choices and technological evolution inevitably constrain the future. For decades, the Fed has been operating on a traditional track. And as the monetary architecture shifts from paper-based to protocol-based, this "path" naturally widens. This is a slow and steady process: integrating new competencies into the existing system. It signifies an awareness—to govern the future of the dollar, one must first understand the language that is writing that future.

data.title: Liang Wenfeng's First-Ever Funding Round – DeepSeek V4 Completely Breaks Free from Nvidia
data.description:
This morning, the AI ​​community was in an uproar! DeepSeek, which has never sought funding, has spoken out for the first time: seeking a $10 billion valuation and raising $300 million. With V4 on the horizon, boasting trillions of parameters and backed by Huawei's Ascend, even Huang (Jensen Huang, CEO of Huawei) is worried! This morning, the AI ​​community was shocked by this news. The Information reported that DeepSeek is seeking its first external funding round! Breaking its "never seeking funding" rule, DeepSeek is now seeking at least $300 million at a valuation of at least $10 billion. In the extremely expensive race to develop large AI models, DeepSeek needs to replenish its financial arsenal. DeepSeek, once a staunch advocate of not seeking funding, has repeatedly rejected investment offers from top domestic venture capital firms and tech giants after its R1 round caused a stir in Silicon Valley and Wall Street. This latest fundraising move by DeepSeek signifies a major shift in its management philosophy, as evidenced by Liang Wenfeng's (Huanfang Quant) decision. Previously, as a technological idealist, he had always hoped to maintain DeepSeek's independence, free from commercial pressure. The last time a new generation model was released was in 2025 when DeepSeek R1 became a sensation. Now, DeepSeek has kept the entire industry waiting for a year and a half. If this funding round is successful, DeepSeek can acquire more computing power and offer higher salaries to prevent the loss of top researchers. However, due to DeepSeek's status as a "Chinese startup," some American venture capitalists are more cautious. The release of DeepSeek V4 has been repeatedly delayed, and the competitive landscape of the AI ​​industry has changed dramatically. Currently, the world's leading models are rapidly iterating, and tech giants from China and the US are leveraging their deep pockets to continuously gain a leading position. Perhaps this pressure has finally prompted DeepSeek to change its funding strategy.

V4, a tough battle with trillions of parameters

According to The Information, V4 was originally scheduled to debut in February of this year, but has been delayed multiple times. Reuters' latest timeframe in early April is "within the next few weeks."

V4, a tough battle with trillions of parameters

Based on currently available information, V4's scale and ambition far surpass its predecessor. The number of parameters has jumped to the trillion level. V4 adopts the MoE architecture, with a total of approximately one trillion parameters, but each token only activates about 37 billion parameters, keeping inference costs on par with V3. This design philosophy continues DeepSeek's consistent efficiency-first approach. The context window of the leaked image from the source network has been expanded to 1 million tokens. V4 introduces a conditional memory architecture called Engram, achieving constant-time retrieval of extremely long contexts. According to internal testing, the information recall rate reached 97% with a token length of 1 million, far exceeding V3's performance with 128K contexts. Native multimodal. According to the FT, V4 will be DeepSeek's first native multimodal model, supporting text, image, and video generation. Previously, DeepSeek's models were primarily text-based, while other flagship models had already embraced multimodal capabilities. Significantly enhanced code generation capabilities. From its inception, V4 has focused on code generation as a core objective. Internal benchmarks show SWE-bench scores exceeding 80% and HumanEval scores reaching 90%. It's claimed that V4 can handle complex bug fixes at the entire codebase level, and its performance in long-context code inference will surpass the Claude and GPT series. Furthermore, there are reports that V4 is expected to be released in two versions: a full version with over a trillion parameters, optimized for advanced inference and complex code tasks, targeting Huawei's Ascend chip; and a lightweight version with approximately 200 billion parameters, targeting general dialogue and API services, capable of running on other domestically produced chips. Regarding open source, V4 is planned to be released under the Apache 2.0 license, continuing DeepSeek's consistent open-source strategy. When will it be available? Multiple sources point to the end of April. On March 9th, a version called "V4 Lite" briefly appeared on the DeepSeek platform before being removed. In early April, developers discovered a test version of V4 Lite on API nodes, showing a 30% improvement in inference speed and a jump in information recall rate from 45% to 94% for 128K context. DeepSeek recently posted job openings for server maintenance engineers and delivery managers in Ulanqab, Inner Mongolia. This marks the company's first public recruitment of on-site personnel related to computing infrastructure. All indications suggest that V4 is no longer in the lab but is undergoing final preparations for large-scale deployment. The thing Huang (DeepSeek's founder) least wanted to see is happening.
